title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole
description: Mole Negro is the crown jewel of Oaxacan cuisine, a rich, complex, and deeply flavorful black mole renowned for its intricate balance of smoky chiles, aromatic spices, nuts, seeds, and bittersweet chocolate. This labor of love yields a velvety, dark sauce with layers of sweet, savory, and subtly spicy notes, perfect for special occasions.

ingredients.notes: Dried, specifically Pasilla Oaxaca if available, otherwise regular Pasilla

instructions.title: Prepare and Rehydrate the Chiles
instructions.text: Heat a comal or heavy skillet over medium-low heat. Toast each dried chile (ancho, mulato, pasilla, chipotle) individually for 15-30 seconds per side, until fragrant but not burnt. Remove stems and seeds. Place toasted chiles in a bowl, cover with boiling hot water, and let them rehydrate for at least 20-30 minutes, until soft. Drain, reserving some soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Toast Nuts, Seeds, and Spices
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, pumpkin seeds, and sesame seeds separately until lightly golden and fragrant. Remove and set aside. Then, toast the whole spices (cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, cumin seeds, coriander seeds, anise seeds) until aromatic, about 1-2 minutes. Grind the toasted whole spices along with the dried thyme, marjoram, and oregano in a molcajete or spice grinder until finely powdered. Set aside.

instructions.title: Fry Aromatics and Thickeners
instructions.text: Heat 1/4 cup of lard (or oil) in a large Dutch oven or heavy pot over medium heat. Fry the chopped onion and garlic until softened and lightly browned, about 5-7 minutes. Add the halved tomatoes and tomatillos, cooking until softened and slightly charred, about 8-10 minutes. Remove vegetables and set aside. In the same pot, add a little more lard if needed, then fry the plantain slices until golden brown. Remove and set aside. Next, fry the torn stale tortillas and bread until crispy. Remove and set aside. Lastly, quickly fry the raisins and prunes for about 1 minute until plump. Remove and set aside.

instructions.title: Assemble and Grind the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Combine all the prepared ingredients: rehydrated chiles, toasted nuts and seeds, fried vegetables (onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomatillos), fried plantain, fried tortillas and bread, fried raisins and prunes, and the ground spices. Working in batches, blend these ingredients with enough hot chicken broth (and some reserved chile soaking liquid if needed) until a very smooth, thick paste forms. This may take several minutes per batch. Strain the paste through a fine-mesh sieve or colander to remove any remaining chile skins or fibrous bits, pushing through with a spoon. This step is crucial for a smooth mole.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Base
instructions.text: Heat the remaining 1/4 cup of lard (or oil) in the same Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully add the strained mole paste, stirring constantly to prevent sticking. This is a critical step, as the mole must 'fry' for at least 20-30 minutes, stirring frequently, until it darkens significantly and thickens, reducing in volume. This frying develops the deep flavors. Gradually stir in 4-5 cups of hot chicken broth, a little at a time, until the mole reaches a desired consistency (like a thick soup). Add the Mexican chocolate, stirring until it is completely melted and incorporated.

instructions.title: Simmer and Season
instructions.text: Reduce heat to low and simmer the mole, uncovered, for at least 1.5 to 2 hours, stirring occasionally to prevent scorching. The mole will continue to darken and its flavors will meld and deepen. If it becomes too thick, add more hot chicken broth, a little at a time, until it reaches your desired consistency. Season generously with salt to taste. If the mole is too bitter or acidic, a pinch or two of sugar can be added to balance the flavors. The mole should be rich, velvety, and balanced.

instructions.title: Final Touches and Serving
instructions.text: Once the mole has simmered sufficiently and the flavors are perfectly balanced, it's ready to serve. Traditionally, Mole Negro is served with tender pieces of cooked chicken or turkey, rice, and warm corn tortillas. Garnish with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds if desired. The mole's flavor will often improve the next day, making it an excellent dish to prepare in advance.

tips: **Chile Selection:** For the most authentic flavor, seek out Chilhuacle Negro chiles, though they are rare outside of Oaxaca. The combination of Ancho, Mulato, and Pasilla Oaxaca is a superb and more accessible alternative.

**Thorough Blending & Straining:** Do not skip the straining step. A truly authentic Mole Negro has a silky smooth texture, which is achieved by passing the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve or a chino.

**The 'Frying' of the Mole:** The initial frying of the mole paste in lard is crucial. This process, known as 'sazonar' or 'freír la pasta', cooks out the raw flavors of the chiles and spices, intensifying and darkening the mole's color and depth of flavor.

**Patience is Key:** Mole Negro is a labor of love. The long simmering time allows all the complex flavors to meld and mature. Don't rush it; the extra time is well worth it for an unparalleled depth of taste.
article:
## History of Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

Mole Negro is arguably the most iconic and revered of Oaxaca's seven moles, often referred to as 'the king of moles.' Its origins are deeply rooted in pre-Hispanic culinary traditions, with ancient Mesoamerican peoples already combining chiles, seeds, and spices. The arrival of the Spanish introduced new ingredients like nuts, dried fruits, and chocolate, which were gradually incorporated to create the complex sauce we know today. Monasteries in colonial Mexico are often credited with refining many mole recipes, including Mole Negro, as nuns had access to a wide array of ingredients and the leisure to experiment. This mole represents a culinary fusion, blending indigenous ingredients and techniques with European additions, resulting in a dish of profound cultural and historical significance.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe achieves its authentic depth by meticulously layering flavors through distinct preparation stages. Toasting the chiles, nuts, and spices individually maximizes their aromatic potential without burning. Frying the vegetables, fruits, and starches (like plantain and tortillas) adds rich, caramelized notes and acts as a natural thickener. The painstaking process of blending and straining ensures an incredibly smooth, luxurious texture. Crucially, the long 'frying' of the mole paste in lard, followed by an extended simmer, allows the myriad ingredients to meld into a harmonious, complex sauce where no single flavor dominates, but rather a symphony of smoky, sweet, savory, and earthy notes emerges. The bittersweet Mexican chocolate provides the iconic dark color and a balancing counterpoint to the chiles and spices.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To elevate your Mole Negro, always use fresh, high-quality dried chiles; older chiles will lack flavor. While the recipe specifies common chiles, if you can source Chilhuacle Negro chiles, they are the cornerstone of true Oaxacan Mole Negro and will significantly impact the flavor profile. Don't underestimate the importance of the initial frying of the mole paste; this step develops the deep, rich color and profound taste. Use a heavy-bottomed pot to prevent scorching during the long simmer, and stir frequently. Adjust the consistency with hot chicken broth gradually, aiming for a thick, velvety sauce that coats the back of a spoon. Taste and adjust seasoning often; a touch of sugar can balance bitterness, and salt is vital to bring out all the nuanced flavors.

## Serving Suggestions

Mole Negro is traditionally served with poultry, most commonly chicken or turkey, often poached or roasted until tender. Shredded chicken bathed in the mole sauce, accompanied by fluffy white rice to absorb the rich flavors, is a classic presentation. For a truly Oaxacan experience, serve with warm, freshly made corn tortillas, which are perfect for scooping up every last drop of the exquisite sauce. It can also be served with pork, particularly slow-cooked carnitas, or even as a vegetarian option over roasted vegetables or tamales. Garnish with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds for added texture and visual appeal, and perhaps a sprig of fresh cilantro.

recipeIngredient: 4 Pasilla Chiles (Dried, specifically Pasilla Oaxaca if available, otherwise regular Pasilla)
2 Chipotle Chiles (Dried, for a smoky kick)
50g (1.7 oz) Mexican Chocolate (Tableta de chocolate, bittersweet)
